Architectural Vision

The Restored Court

“He dreamed of Racquet Courts with distinctive peaked ceilings, glass ceilings where members could play under sun and stars.”

— On Martin Roche’s 1909 design

AI rendering: The restored UCC racquets court, viewed from the spectators’ gallery

Two players compete in all whites beneath the peaked timber ceiling. Spectators watch from the viewing gallery above the back wall. The red telltale board marks the base of the front wall — the defining feature of every racquets court in the world.

The Floor Rediscovered — 2026

Hidden in Plain Sight

During a routine gym floor renovation in 2026, workers removed the raised rubber flooring to reveal the original 1909 stone court floor — complete with red-painted court markings still clearly visible after more than 100 years.

Original red court line, perfectly preserved beneath the gym floor
Service boxes and short line markings emerge as gym flooring is removed
The full court floor exposed — red markings, green gym tiles stacked against the wall, university pennants above

The court isn’t just a memory. The bones are here. The floor is here. The markings are here. It’s waiting to be played on again.

Origins & Gameplay

The Sport of Racquets

Racquets is an exhilarating racquet sport which gave birth to squash and is actively played in the UK, US, and Canada. With the ball travelling more than 150 mph, it is one of the fastest ball sports in the world.

150+
MPH Ball Speed
1st to 15
Points to Win
30.5″
Wooden Racquet
1.5″
Hard White Ball
Fleet Debtors’ Prison, London — where racquets was first played in the 18th century
Origins
First played in the 18th century against the open walls of debtors’ prisons in England — the King’s Bench and the Fleet
Popular outside prisons by the early 1800s, with courts at taverns across London, Bristol, Bath, Birmingham, and Belfast
Mid-19th century: covered courts dominated. The MCC and Prince’s Club built courts.
Introduced worldwide by the British Army — Shanghai, Gibraltar, Malta, India, Burma, the Caribbean
Became a game for the wealthy at private country houses, clubs, and prestigious schools including Harrow (1865 court still in use), Eton, and Winchester
Introduced to North America by the British Army, with courts in New York, Chicago, Boston, Philadelphia, Detroit, St. Louis, and Montreal
How It’s Played
Games are played first to 15. Only the server can score — the receiver must win the rally to earn the serve.
Server serves from either service box. US: one serve only. UK: second serve allowed.
Serve must hit above the service line on the front wall. Subsequent shots must hit above the telltale (tin).
Ball may bounce once on the floor but can hit any wall — leading to extensive use of angles and boasting.
Harry Boakes & the UCC

Great players like Harry Boakes Jr., F.F. Rolland, Clarence C. Pell, and Bobby Grant III emerged in North America. Harry Boakes Sr. served as the Racquets professional at the University Club during the late 1910s and early 1920s. Countless articles in Chicago local papers recount his accomplishments.

Where Racquets Is Played Today

A Global Game

Racquets is played in 36 active courts worldwide, maintained exclusively by private clubs and elite schools.

36
Active Courts
28
UK Courts
8
North American
16
UK Schools
United Kingdom — 28 Courts

16 elite schools maintain 24 courts: Charterhouse, Cheltenham, Clifton, Eton, Haileybury, Harrow, Malvern, Marlborough, Radley, Rugby, St. Paul’s, Tonbridge, RMA Sandhurst, BRNC Dartmouth, Wellington, and Winchester. Three private clubs maintain 4 courts: Queen’s Club London, Seacourt Tennis Club, and Manchester T&R Club.

Queen’s Club, London
The leading racquet sports club globally
Hosts the Wimbledon grass court championship warm-up tournament
10-year waitlist with 4,000+ active members
Facilities include lawn tennis, two court tennis courts, three squash courts, two padel courts, and two racquets courts
United States & Canada — 8 Courts

Racquet Club of Chicago (IL), Detroit Racquet Club (MI), Racquet & Tennis Club (NY), Union Club (NY), Tuxedo Club (NY), Harvard Club (Boston, MA), Racquet Club of Philadelphia (PA), and Montreal Racquets Club (Canada).

Governing Bodies & Organizations
North American Racquets Association (NARA)
Tennis & Rackets Association (T&RA)
Thomas C. Shumaker Racquets Foundation
The Jesters Club (North America & Europe)
Growing the Game
The UK produces hundreds of young racquets athletes across 28 courts at 16 elite schools annually
In the US & Canada, 7 clubs with 8 courts use their programs to convert squash players to racquets
The number of female players has exploded in recent years and is projected to keep growing
History & Opportunity

Racquets in Chicago & the UCC

Chicago’s Racquets History — 7 Courts Built
University Club of Chicago — 2 courts (inactive)
Illinois Athletic Club — 1 court (demolished)
Chicago Athletic Association — 1 court (demolished)
University of Chicago — 1 court (demolished)
Racquet Club of Chicago — 2 courts (active today)
Envisioning the Past

The 12th Floor, c. 1909

Martin Roche’s original 12th floor vision — racquets courts, skylights, and roof garden

“This drawing presents Martin Roche’s sense of flair in creating destinations like no other in Chicago. He imagined a place one could experience the great southern expanse of Lake Michigan, view the beauty of Chicago’s lakefront from a unique vantage point. He dreamed of Racquet Courts with distinctive peaked ceilings, glass ceilings where members could play under sun and stars.”

Two enormous skylights in the original roof framing plans
Court #1 current state — annotated with original features
What Survives Today
Unmolested interior court walls (south wall fully intact upon inspection)
Front wall with “Special Steel Door”
Dual roofline skylights identified in original roof framing plans
Gallery entrance visible, with clues identifying gallery boundaries
Original stone court floor with red markings discovered during 2026 gym renovation

The UCC can increase national participation by converting its skilled squash athletes into racquets players — and with the Racquet Club of Chicago less than 2 miles away, establish new traditions and competition weekends.

Why Restore the Court

Members’ Benefits

Unique Sporting Experience

Offering racquets sets the club apart, giving members the opportunity to engage with a vibrant global community and learn the fastest racquet sport on earth.

Revenue Generation

A well-organized racquets program generates additional income through memberships, court rentals, overnight guests, lessons, and hosting tournaments.

Reclaimed Prestige

Racquets courts are rare, coveted venues that afford special bonds between prestigious North American and European clubs — ties lost since closing our courts.

Social Connection

A racquets program fosters friendships and new ties among members. Members who make friends don’t resign easily.

Heritage & Pride

Racquets was part of the UCC’s original 1909 design. Reintroducing it restores a lost connection to our past and bolsters club pride.

Enhanced Reputation

Commitment to diverse, high-quality amenities enhances the UCC’s image and attracts positive attention from the broader community.

Culture & Community

Traditions of the Game

Tournament Culture
Annual tournaments hosted by sister clubs in the US, UK, and Canada
A global community that regularly travels worldwide to compete and socialize
Sister clubs welcome players on an informal reciprocal basis
Tournaments extend over multiple days — formal meals, cocktail parties, events at host clubs and member homes
Weekends include golf, other racket sports, sightseeing, and cultural opportunities
The Quintathlon

A bi-annual competition bringing the racquets world together to compete in racquets, court tennis, squash, lawn tennis, and at least one other sport. The 2024 edition spanned Tuxedo Park and New York City over six days — cocktail kickoff, golf, racket sports, a Mets game, and a formal closing dinner.

Scope & Budget

12th Floor Project

Estimated Investment
$1M – $2M

*Subject to change based on pricing exercises and scope

Restore — Reimagine — Create
1. Racquets court
2. Women’s locker room ($4M budgeted)
3. Player’s lounge
4. Viewing gallery
5. Weight room relocation
6. Corridor refresh
Timing & Coordination
Restoration should begin simultaneously with the Women’s Locker Room for cost savings and to mitigate disruptions
Displaced weight room relocates to the current women’s locker room footprint
Women’s locker room displaces squash courts 5 & 6, leaving old footprint for the weight room
Precedent

Case Study: Racquet Club of Chicago

In 2012, the Racquet Club of Chicago completed a world-class court tennis restoration — bringing a court back to life after more than 75 years.

Newly restored court
Refreshed viewing gallery
Renovated players’ lounge
The Court Tennis Story
1924: Court tennis court opened at the Racquet Club
1936: Fell out of popularity; converted to indoor lawn tennis
Debate about restoration raged for 10 years between opinionated groups
2011: A group of young members won the debate and began organized efforts
Board of Governors voted unanimously to proceed
July 31, 2012: Fully restored court reopened after 75+ years
Results
Converted hundreds of squash and racquets players into court tennis enthusiasts
Hosts tournaments attracting members from the US, Canada, and Europe
People worldwide travel to Chicago for the Racquet Club’s facilities
Today staffed by 4 pros specializing in Racquets, Court Tennis, and Squash
Moving Forward

Suggested Next Steps

1

Visit the Racquet Club

Organize a series of visits to the Racquet Club of Chicago to view facilities, watch gameplay, and learn about the game today.

2

Establish a Committee

Vote to establish a Racquets Court Restoration Committee.

Committee Responsibilities
Manage communications and project development
Source experts
Work in lockstep with Women’s Locker Room Committee to maximize 12th floor potential
Develop floorplans and project timeline
Complete multiple bid cost estimates
Produce design renderings for club leadership
Fund-raising campaigns
Membership outreach in partnership with UCC staff
Oversee racquets program development with UCC athletics staff
